author: Frizlab

title: Some pkgx love?
author: davdroman
body:
I'm bummed out scripting support is still so primitive for Swift in 2025.

Any chance for pkgx to take some ideas from `swift-sh` and introduce them into the `mash` ecosystem? I'd love to be able to write the following standalone script and have it work:

```swift
#!/usr/bin/env -S pkgx swift@6

import PromiseKit //! @mxcl ~> 6.5

print(Promise.value("Hi!"))
```

Dependency decl syntax up to brainstorming of course.

title: Swift SH can't clone repo
author: iharandreyev
body:
When running my script I get an error

```
error: Failed to clone repository https://github.com/mxcl/CryptoSwift.git:

    fatal: repository 'https://github.com/mxcl/CryptoSwift.git/' not found
```

Fetching packages from Xcode works just fine
I use SSH for all my Xcode projects
I do realize that this is a configuration problem on my particular machine

Please help

title: Hyphen or Space?
author: jradxl
body:
I'm coming from Linux experience and have been investigating whether I can use Swiftlang for general scripting instead of Bash.
I came across this repo, but the documentation is very confusing as you write "swift sh" in most places.

I'm using 5.10.1, and swift-sh builds without issue.
I copy  `.build/debug/swift-sh` to `/usr/local/bin` as I would expect

I then use the Promise Example as follows:

```
$ cat script 
#!/usr/bin/env swift-sh
import PromiseKit  // @mxcl ~> 6.5
print(Promise.value("Hi!"))
```

Please note I use "swift-sh" as based on my Linux experience, "swift sh" does not work.
My script above works fine, and I am very impressed.

Are these "swift sh"  un-noticed typos, or is there something else I should know.

title: Missing support for async entry points
author: dimitribouniol
body:
It doesn't seem like async entry points work when using `swift sh`, at least at first glance. I initially noticed this when migrating from `ParsableCommand` to `AsyncParsableCommand`, and all of a sudden my command only printed usage information, but never ran. Unfortunately, adding an `await` to the entry point `await YourCommand.main()` doesn't work (non-`async` `main()` is preferred), though the likely proper solution is to implement something like in #163, but make sure async entry points are supported.

For those that land here, there is a workaround:
```
let group = DispatchGroup()
group.enter()
Task {
    await YourCommand.main()
    group.leave()
}
group.wait()
```

title: error: The operation couldn’t be completed. (NSPOSIXErrorDomain.13)

```

```

title: Package not regenerated if you change the import reference
author: JetForMe
body:
I can't remember exactly how I got into this state, but I had done `swift sh edit <my script`. Then in Xcode I added

```swift
import Path // @mxcl ~> 1.4.0`
```

but it couldn't fetch the dependency. I re-read the docs, and saw I needed

```swift
import Path // mxcl/Path.swift ~> 1.4.0
```

(and the first time I tried this, I used `@mxcl/Path.swift`, not sure if that contributed).

Anyway, long story short, it seems once the project was open in Xcode, making changes to the comment and running either in Xcode or on the command line (with `./my-script.sh`) didn't update things, and it couldn't find the dependency.

Once I did `swift sh edit my-script.sh`, it finally updated Package.swift to reflect the proper URL.

*UPDATE:* No, I take it back. It updated the Xcode package, but I still can't run it from the terminal:

```swift
$ ./inst-rom.sh 
inst-rom.sh:3:8: error: no such module 'Path'
import Path                             // https://github.com/mxcl/Path.swift.git ~> 1.4.0
       ^
```

I deleted the `swift-sh` cache directory entirely and tried executing my script on the command line, and it doesn't work. Is there another cache somewhere?

```bash
$ ./inst-rom.sh 
inst-rom.sh:3:8: error: no such module 'Path'
import Path                             // mxcl/Path.swift ~> 1.4.0
       ^
```

```swift
#!/usr/bin/env swift

import Path				// mxcl/Path.swift ~> 1.4.0


print("hello world \(Path.cwd)")
```

title: Configurable templates/context
author: wti
body:
It's likely a pain to track swift/swiftpm versions and user packaging desires.

Using extensible templates and context might help.

The core workflow of swift-sh works great, but the dependency parsing and Package.swift generation is static while swift/swiftpm versions and user needs grow and vary.  To support existing users, swift-sh targets the oldest version possible, which constrains features.
 
If/since Package.swift is a function of the template and context variables, making each of those extensible/configurable should help, and may drive features. Users would use external templates, and then contribute them to swift-sh to include in resources as available templates triggered by higher-level flags/conditions (like tools-version).

This is to propose initial steps to template-generation, with minimal CLI support for extending context, but
- no changes to parsing
- no/minimal changes to existing default code path for the interpolated template

## Context & CLI
- Support context specified on the command-line (in the shebang line)
- Context initially just -Dname=value pairs
- Template flag with script-relative template path

## Templates
1. `Script.write()` can migrate to a static method taking the script package metadata and template configuration.  
2. The default is the current string interpolation, but configuration can specify any template.  
3. Unit tests can target the static method, e.g., comparing vetted and new variants

### Design/staging plan, to review and agree...
1. Settle on a template facility (homegrown or library - Stencil?)
2. Review/modify the script metadata for usability in templates and extensibility via command-line
    - Keep current parser, just repackage metadata if needed
    - The model is detailed but constrained by swiftpm; dependency versions are a PITA to avoid for now
3. Integrate
4. Test

Some non-required features to consider hoisting for testability:
- template dir: local template access by name relative to dir
- preflight mode to generate but not build

Testing:
- CLI, context parsing, and template generation can all be unit tested
- integration tests using all available context, minimum viable context, and (error case for) missing context

Nice to have:
- Demo package for user script template proposals

Constraints:
- Support the present behavior exactly when unconfigured, using no new code paths.  Users must opt in to new code.
- No changes to context available via parsing (initially)
- My time is pretty limited, so roll out incrementally with optional/opt-in CLI parameters, working in branch with milestone and releasable tags

Please let me know if you have time/interest/schedule for settling on a plan and reviewing work in branch.

title: Editing using `swift sh edit` eventually fails with "The file doesn't exist"
author: itsthejb
body: Hi, Very interesting tool here, and trying to use it. However, when attempting to edit a script using `swift sh edit `, Xcode will quickly begin complaining that the file being edited _actually doesn't exist_ (even though it does): Once this happens, Xcode goes into a death spiral where there's not much you can do apart from kill it. And then it'll still re-open the project and fail to access the file... I've seen this a few times when experimenting with scripting, and was surprised to see it also happening with this tool. It seems to be something wrong with Xcode (14.1), although has happened previously. Since I'd love to be able to script, posting here for ideas, and to see if anyone else has experienced this. Thanks!

title: CI and test failures update & PR links
author: wti
body:
One PR should get tests passing in CI, and another to pass tests locally in 5.6+:

- Cards n/a via github
    - [184](https://github.com/mxcl/swift-sh/pull/184) replaces cards with Ink
    - After this, CI should work on old versions
    - Tests will still fail locally for developers running Swift > 5.5
- Swift versions > 5.5
    - [181](https://github.com/mxcl/swift-sh/pull/181) fixes version output snooping, expected values 5.6...5.9
    - After this, local developer tests should pass for Swift 5.5..5.7
 
Not sure if you want to update [ci.yml](https://github.com/mxcl/swift-sh/blob/master/.github/workflows/ci.yml) with versions 5.6 and 5.7 and/or macOS 12 and 13.

title: Expose an option that prints the cached build directory?
author: frypf
body:
I've been doing some swift scripting in VSCode rather than Xcode for a change and it would be really useful if there was some way of outputting the path of the build dir generated within `~/Library/Developer/swift-sh.cache`. I had a quick look at [Path+ResolvedHash.swift](https://github.com/mxcl/swift-sh/blob/aae4735cca0dd79160bd5781b0ff59633f0a7184/Sources/Utility/Path+ResolvedHash.swift) and hacked together a short script (obviously utilising the capabilities of `swift-sh` 😁) to access the generated `md5` for a given file.

However, this involves a fair bit of overhead, as well as c. 138mb of generated data just to output a single piece of info that is already calculated internally within `swift-sh`. Would it be possible to add a `--print-build-dir` option (or similar) within `swift-sh` itself? Or maybe this info is already accessible and I've completely missed it!

Thanks for providing such a useful tool 🙏!

title: Error reference to member `v12_1` cannot be resolved
author: Jeehut
body:
I'm on latest stable version of macOS (12.0.1) and Xcode (13.1) and I get this error:
```
~/Library/Developer/swift-sh.cache/0f52af8da2fce590923c596de278045a: error: manifest parse error(s):
~/Library/Developer/swift-sh.cache/0f52af8da2fce590923c596de278045a/Package.swift:24:13: error: reference to member 'v12_1' cannot be resolved without a contextual type
    .macOS(.v12_1)
```

Could it be that another update of Swift-SH is needed to fix this?

The error happens when trying to import [AnyLint](https://github.com/Flinesoft/AnyLint) in a script like this:

```Swift
#!/usr/local/bin/swift-sh
import AnyLint // @Flinesoft ~> 0.8.3

// some code using AnyLint
```

I have the latest version `2.3.1` installed. I also tried running `swift-sh --clean-cache` and then my script, same result.

Here's also the full output of `swift --version`:
```
swift-driver version: 1.26.9 Apple Swift version 5.5.1 (swiftlang-1300.0.31.4 clang-1300.0.29.6)
Target: x86_64-apple-macosx12.0
```

title: Dashes in product name breaks dependency resolving
author: philprime
body:
When importing a package which contains dash in the library name, they are automatically replaced by the Xcode build system to underscores, which breaks the dependency resolving and makes them unusable.

Example: the package [AppStoreConnect-Swift-SDK](https://github.com/AvdLee/appstoreconnect-swift-sdk) by @AvdLee

The product name is defined in the [Package.swift](https://github.com/AvdLee/appstoreconnect-swift-sdk/blob/master/Package.swift) as the following:

```swift
...
products: [
    .library(name: "AppStoreConnect-Swift-SDK", targets: ["AppStoreConnect-Swift-SDK"])
],
...
```

which requires the import to be the following:

```swift
import AppStoreConnect_Swift_SDK    // AvdLee/appstoreconnect-swift-sdk
// instead of
import AppStoreConnect-Swift-SDK    // AvdLee/appstoreconnect-swift-sdk
```

as dashes are automatically replaced with `_`.

This results in the following error:

```
'test' /Users/Philip/Library/Developer/swift-sh.cache/1f801762d2aba590bc4b77b62e521b02: error: product 'AppStoreConnect_Swift_SDK' not found in package 'appstoreconnect-swift-sdk'. it is required by package 'test' target 'test'.
warning: dependency 'AppStoreConnect-Swift-SDK' is not used by any target
error: 1 <(/usr/bin/swift build -Xswiftc -suppress-warnings)
```

### Minimal Example to reproduce:

```swift
#!/usr/bin/swift sh

import AppStoreConnect_Swift_SDK    // AvdLee/appstoreconnect-swift-sdk
```

### Ideas to fix the issue:

- add option to define the product name, e.g. 

```swift
#!/usr/bin/swift sh

import AppStoreConnect_Swift_SDK    // AvdLee/appstoreconnect-swift-sdk#appstoreconnect-swift-sdk
```

- inform package authors about the name-convention breaking and provide a duplicated target, e.g.

```swift
products: [
  // original:
  .library(name: "AppStoreConnect-Swift-SDK", targets: ["AppStoreConnect-Swift-SDK"]),
  // new:
  .library(name: "AppStoreConnect_Swift_SDK", targets: ["AppStoreConnect-Swift-SDK"])
],
```

body:

Please report this issue to the mxcl/made tap (not Homebrew/brew or Homebrew/core), or even better, submit a PR to fix it:

> Warning: Calling `sha256 "digest" => :tag` in a bottle block is deprecated! Use `brew style --fix` on the formula to update the style or use `sha256 tag: "digest"` instead.
Please report this issue to the mxcl/made tap (not Homebrew/brew or Homebrew/core), or even better, submit a PR to fix it:

title: CI won't build linux with swift 5.1
author: haikusw
body:
CI won't build current Linux w/ swift 5.1 workflow because of an error.   Reported it and got this reply:

"GitHub bumped the version of Ubuntu that ubuntu-latest points to and older versions of Swift aren't available on that version." 

https://github.com/fwal/setup-swift/issues/202#issuecomment-813999175

So maybe change that workflow to not use linux-latest and point to a specific older version of linux that supports swift 5.1?  Or remove 5.1 linux from the workflows (?).

This came up because I updated a comment on my PR #140 and CI tried to build the change and failed.

title: Fail to import when the package-name and repo-name are not identical
author: xareelee
body:
I use  [swift-sh](https://github.com/mxcl/swift-sh) successfully to import other libs, but not [EonilFSEvents](https://github.com/eonil/FSEvents).

```swift
#!/usr/bin/swift sh

import Foundation
import PromiseKit  // @mxcl ~> 6.5
import EonilFSEvents  // eonil/FSEvents ~> 0.1
```

It shows an error while importing after successfully resolving dependencies:

```
Updating https://github.com/mxcl/PromiseKit.git
Fetching https://github.com/eonil/FSEvents.git
Cloning https://github.com/eonil/FSEvents.git
Resolving https://github.com/eonil/FSEvents.git at 0.1.7
'your-swift-script' path-to/swift-sh.cache/49e96c02cae55802c8b6c24fbe5d3972: error: product dependency 'EonilFSEvents' in package 'FSEvents' not found
error: 1 <(/usr/bin/swift build -Xswiftc -suppress-warnings)
```

After downloading `EonilFSEvents` and trying to load it locally, I find the problem. The package name in `Package.swift` doesn't match the repo name `FSEvents` which `swift-sh` is trying to find:

```swift
// Package.swift in eonil/FSEvents

let package = Package(
    name: "EonilFSEvents",
    ...
)
```

To resolve this problem manually, I need to either:

- change the `name` in `Package.swift` to match its folder name, or
- change the folder name to match the `name` in `Package.swift` 

I think that `swift-sh` should support redirecting the package name (for example, using `#` as below):

```
import EonilFSEvents  // eonil/FSEvents #EonilFSEvents ~> 0.1
// ^^ for when the package-name and repo-name are not identical
```

There are three names that may not be identical:

1. module name: which you import
2. package name: which is defined in `Package.swift`
3. repo name (or folder name): where the repo is
